# Summary

- A bisector of a line segment divides the line segment into two equal parts
- Right bisector cuts the line segment into two equal parts at 90°.
- Any point on the right bisector of a line segment is equidistant from its end points.
- Any point is equidistant from the points of a line segment is on its right bisector.
- The right bisectors of the sides of a triangle are concurrent.
- Any point on the bisector of an angle is equidistant from its arms.
- Any point inside an angle, equidistant from its arms, is on its bisector.
- The bisectors of the angles of a triangle are concurrent.

nodes.title: 12.0 Sides and angles of a triangle
nodes.text:
Unit 12

Weightage = 5%

# SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRAINGLE

## Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs)

After completing this unit, students will be able to:

- If two sides of a triangle are unequal in length, the longer side has an angle of greater measure opposite to it.
- If two angles of a triangle are unequal in measure, the side opposite to the greater angle is longer than the side opposite to the smaller angle.
- The sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the length of the third side.
- From a point, outside a line, the perpendicular is the shortest distance from the point to the line.

![img-12.jpeg](img-12.jpeg)

nodes.title: Theorems and Properties of Triangle Sides and Angles
nodes.summary: This text covers fundamental geometric theorems regarding the relationships between the sides and angles of a triangle. It provides formal proofs for three main theorems: the relationship between unequal sides and their opposite angles, the converse relationship between unequal angles and their opposite sides, and the triangle inequality theorem (the sum of any two sides is greater than the third). Additionally, it includes corollaries for right and obtuse triangles, as well as worked examples and exercises demonstrating how to apply these principles to verify the validity of triangle side lengths.
nodes.text:
UNIT:12 SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE

Page:215

# Introduction

In this unit we will learn the theorems related to the sides and angles of the triangle along with their corollaries and apply them to solve the allied problems.

## 12.1 Sides and Angles of a Triangle

### Theorem 12.1.1

**Prove that:**

If two sides of a triangle are unequal in length, the longer side has an angle of greater measure opposite to it.

**Given:**

In $\Delta ABC$, $m\overline{AC} > m\overline{AB}$.

**To Prove:**

$$
m \angle ABC > m \angle ACB
$$

**Construction:**

On $\overline{AC}$ take a point $D$ such that $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{AB}$.

Join $B$ to $D$ so that $\Delta ADB$ is an isosceles triangle.

Label $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ as shown in the figure.

**Proof:**

|  Statements |   | Reasons  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  In | $\Delta ABD$, $m\angle 1 = m\angle 2$ ... (i) | Angles opposite to congruent sides (construction).  |
|  In | $\Delta BCD$, $m\angle ACB < m\angle 2$ or $m\angle 2 > m\angle ACB$ ... (ii) | (An exterior angle of a triangle is greater than a non-adjacent interior angles)  |
|  ∴ | $m\angle 1 > m\angle ACB$ ... (iii) | By (i) and (ii)  |
|  But | $\angle ABC = m\angle 1 + m\angle DBC$ | Postulate of addition of angles  |
|  ∴ | $m\angle ABC > m\angle 1$ ... (iv) |   |
|  ∴ | $m\angle ABC > m\angle 1 > m\angle ACB$ | By (iii) and (iv)  |
|  Hence | $\angle ABC > m\angle ACB$ | (Transitive property of in-equality of real numbers).  |

![img-13.jpeg](img-13.jpeg)

#

1

#

UNIT:12 SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE

Following example will help to understand the above theorem.

Example: Prove that in a scalene triangle, the angle opposite to the largest side is of measure greater than  $60^{\circ}$ .

![img-14.jpeg](img-14.jpeg)

Given:

In  $\Delta ABC$ , with,  $m\overline{AC} > m\overline{AB}$  and  $m\overline{AC} > m\overline{BC}$ .

To Prove:

$$
m \angle B > 6 0 ^ {0}
$$

Proof:

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  In ΔABC. |   |
|  We have, m∠B > m∠C | mAC > mAB  |
|  and m∠B > m∠A | mAC > mBC  |
|  but, m∠A + m∠B + m∠C = 180° | ∠A, ∠B and ∠C are the angles of the ΔABC.  |
|  ∴ m∠B + m∠B + m∠B > 180° | m∠B > m∠C and m∠B > m∠A  |
|  i.e 3 m∠B > 180° | By addition  |
|  = m∠B > 180°/3 | Dividing both sides by 3  |
|  Thus, m∠B > 60° |   |

Q.E.D

UNIT-12 SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE

# Theorem 12.1.2

Prove that if two angles of a triangle are unequal in measure, the side opposite to the greater angle is longer than the side opposite to the smaller angle.

Given:

$$
\ln \Delta \mathrm {A B C}, m \angle \mathrm {B} > m \angle \mathrm {C}
$$

To Prove:

$$
\overline {{m \overline {{A C}}}} > \overline {{m \overline {{A B}}}}
$$

Construction:

![img-15.jpeg](img-15.jpeg)

Make  $\angle ABM \cong \angle C$ . Draw  $\overline{BN}$ , the bisector of  $\angle MBC$ , i.e.

$$
m \angle 1 = m \angle 2.
$$

Proof:

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  ∠ANB is the exterior ∠ of ΔCBN | By definition of exterior ∠  |
|  ∴ m∠ANB = m∠C + m∠2 | ∴ m∠2 = m∠1(Construction)  |
|  = m∠C + m∠1 | ∴ m∠C = m∠ABM(Construction)  |
|  = m∠ABM + m∠1 | By angle addition postulate  |
|  = m∠ABN | ∴ m∠ANB = m∠ABN (Proved above)  |
|  ∴ AB ≡ AN | ∴ mAC > mAN  |
|  ∴ mAC > mAB |   |

# Q.E.D

# Corollaries:

1. The hypotenuse of a right angle is longer than each of the other two sides.
2. In an obtuse angled triangle, the side opposite to the obtuse angle is longer than each of the other two sides.

218
UNIT-12
SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE

# Theorem 12.1.3

The sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the length of the third side.

Given:

$\Delta ABC$

To Prove:

i)  $m\overline{AB} + m\overline{AC} > m\overline{BC}$
ii)  $m\overline{AB} + m\overline{BC} > m\overline{CA}$
iii)  $m\overline{AC} + m\overline{BC} > m\overline{AB}$

Construction:

Produce  $\overrightarrow{BA}$  to  $D$ , making  $\overrightarrow{AD} \cong \overrightarrow{AC}$ . Draw  $\overrightarrow{DC}$

Proof:

![img-16.jpeg](img-16.jpeg)

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  In ΔADC, $\overrightarrow{AD} \cong \overrightarrow{AC}$ | Construction  |
|  ∴ $m\angle 1 = m\angle 2$ | Angles opposite to congruent sides  |
|  But $m\angle BCD > m\angle 1$ | $m\angle BCD = m\angle BCA + m\angle 1$  |
|  ∴ $m\angle BCD > m\angle 2$ | Transitive property of inequality  |
|  ∴ In ΔBDC, $m\overline{BD} > m\overline{BC}$ ... (i) | Grater angle has greater side opposite to it.  |
|  But $m\overline{BD} = m\overline{AB} + m\overline{AD}$ | By construction  |
|  = $m\overline{AB} + m\overline{AC}$ | $m\overline{AD} = m\overline{AC}$  |
|  ∴ $m\overline{AB} + m\overline{AC} > m\overline{BC}$ | Putting value of $\overrightarrow{BD}$ in (i)  |
|  Similarly, we can prove that: $m\overline{AB} + m\overline{BC} > m\overline{AC}$ | By the above process  |
|  and $m\overline{BC} + m\overline{AC} > m\overline{AB}$ |   |

Q.E.D

The following example will help to understand the above theorem.

nodes.title: Triangle Properties, Theorems, and Practical Geometry
nodes.summary: This text covers the fundamental properties of triangles, including the triangle inequality theorem and the proof that a perpendicular segment is the shortest distance from a point to a line. It provides practical examples for verifying triangle formation, exercises for applying side-angle relationships, and a summary of core geometric theorems. Additionally, it introduces Unit 13, which focuses on the practical construction of triangles, angle bisectors, altitudes, and area-equivalent geometric shapes.
nodes.text:
UNIT:12 SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE
219

## Example 01
Which of the following sets of lengths of the sides form a triangle:
(i) 3 cm, 4 cm and 5 cm
(ii) 4 cm, 5 cm and 4.5 cm
(iii) 60 mm, 80 mm and 10 cm
(iv) 3 cm, 4 cm and 10 cm

### Solution:
(i) 3 cm, 4 cm and 5 cm
Since, $3 + 4 > 5$, $3 + 5 > 4$ and $4 + 5 > 3$
∴ the sum of the two sides of greater than the $3^{\text{rd}}$ side.
Thus, the given set of lengths form a triangle.
(ii) 4 cm, 5 cm and 4.5 cm
Since, $4 + 5 > 4.5$, $5 + 4.5 > 4$ and $4.5 + 3 > 4$
Thus, the given set of lengths form a triangle.
(iii) 60 mm, 80 mm and 10 cm
Since, $10 \text{ mm} = 1$ cm so, $60 \text{ mm} = 6$ cm and $80 \text{ mm} = 8$ cm
Now, $6 + 8 > 10$, $6 + 10 > 8$ and also $8 + 10 > 6$
Thus, the given set of lengths form a triangle.

## Example 02
By using the idea of the above theorem decide, which of the following sets of lengths of the sides form a triangle:
(i) 2 cm, 4 cm and 7 cm
(ii) 5.5 cm, 5 cm and 9.5 cm

### Solution:
(i) 2 cm, 4 cm and 7 cm
Since, $2 + 4 < 7$, $4 + 7 > 2$ and $7 + 2 > 4$
Thus, this type of set of lengths cannot form a triangle.
(ii) 5.5 cm, 5 cm and 9.5 cm
Since, $5.5 + 5 > 9.5$, $5 + 9.5 > 5.5$ and $9.5 + 5.5 > 5$
Thus, the given set of lengths form a triangle.

### Activity
If $a = 3 \text{ cm}$
$b = 4 \text{ cm}$
$c = 5 \text{ cm}$
then $\Delta ABC$ can be formed or not.

#

1

#

UNIT:32 SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE

# Theorem 12.1.4: Prove that:

From a point, outside a line, the perpendicular is the shortest distance from the point to the line.

Given:

From a point  $C$ ,  $\overline{CD}$  is drawn perpendicular to  $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$  meeting it in  $D$  and  $\overline{CE}$  is any other segment meeting  $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$  in  $E$ .

To Prove:

$$
m \overline {{\mathrm {C D}}} <   m \overline {{\mathrm {C E}}}
$$

![img-17.jpeg](img-17.jpeg)

Proof:

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  ∠1 is an exterior ∠ of ΔCDE ∴ m∠1 > m∠3 | By definition of exterior ∠ Exterior ∠ is greater than non-adjacent interior ∠  |
|  ∴ m∠2 > m∠3 | m∠1 = m∠2(right ∠s)  |
|  ∴ mCE > mCD | Side opposite to greater angle  |
|  Similarly, it can be proved that mCD is less than any other segment drawn from C to AB | By the above process  |

Q.E.D

# Corollaries:

1. The distance between a line and point (on a line) is zero.

UNIT-12 SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE
221

## Exercise 12.1

1.  O is an interior point of the $\Delta ABC$.

Show that: $m\overline{OA} + m\overline{OB} + m\overline{OC} > \frac{1}{2}(m\overline{AB} + m\overline{BC} + m\overline{CA})$.

2.  In $\Delta ABC$, $m\angle B = 70^\circ$ and $m\angle C = 45^\circ$. Which of the sides of the triangle is longest?

3.  In $\Delta ABC$, $m\angle A = 55^\circ$ and $m\angle B = 65^\circ$, which of the side of the triangle is smallest?

## Review Exercise 12

1.  Tick (✓) True or False from the following statements.

(i)   Sum of the two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side. T/F

(ii)   The difference of two sides of a triangle is larger than the third side. T/F

(iii)   Perpendicular distance from a point to line is the longest distance between them. T/F

(iv)   In a right angled triangle the largest angle is of $100^\circ$. T/F

(v)   A perpendicular on a line always makes and angle of $90^\circ$. T/F

2.  Fill in the blanks to make the sentences true sentences.

(i)   In any right angled triangle, ______ is the longest side of the triangle.

(ii)   In a right angled triangle, sum of the measures of the sides containing right angles is ______ than the measure of the hypotenuse.

(iii)   In $\Delta ABC$, $m\angle A = 50^\circ$ and $m\angle B = 30^\circ$. Side ______ will be longer than its other sides.

(iv)   Length of diagonal of any quadrilateral is ______ than the sum of the measures of its two adjacent sides.

222
UNIT-32
SIDES AND ANGLES OF A TRIANGLE

3. Tick (✓) the correct answer.

(i) Measure of one side of an equilateral triangle is 6 cm, then the length of its median is ______ 9cm.

a) less than
b) greater than
c) equal to
d) none of the above

(ii) Perimeter of a rectangle is 22cm, then the length of its diagonal is ______ 11cm.

a) equal to
b) greater than
c) less than
d) none of the above

**Summary**

- If two sides of a triangle are unequal in length, the longer side has an angle of greater measure opposite to it.
- If two angles of a triangle are unequal in measure, the side opposite to the greater angle is longer than the side opposite to the smaller angle.
- The sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the length of the third side.
- From a point, outside a line, the perpendicular is the shortest distance from the point to the line.

nodes.title: 13.0 Practical Geometry - Triangles
nodes.summary: This text covers the practical geometry of triangles, detailing step-by-step construction methods for various given parameters, including two sides with an included angle, one side with two angles, and the ambiguous cases involving two sides and an opposite angle. It also provides instructions for drawing angle bisectors and outlines further geometric constructions such as altitudes, medians, perpendicular bisectors, and area-equivalent shapes (rectangles and squares).
nodes.text:
Unit 13

Weightage = 3%

# PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

## Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs)

After completing this unit, students will be able to:

- Construct a triangle having given:
- Two sides and the included angle,
- One side and two of the angles,
- Two of its sides and the angle opposite to one of them, (with all the three possibilities)

- Draw:
- Angle bisectors,
- Altitudes,
- Perpendicular bisectors,
- Medians of a given triangle and verify their given concurrency

- Construct a triangle equal in area to a given quadrilateral.
- Construct a rectangle equal in area to a given triangle.
- Construct a square equal in area to a given rectangle.
- Construct a triangle of equivalent area on a base of given length.

![img-18.jpeg](img-18.jpeg)

![img-19.jpeg](img-19.jpeg)

![img-20.jpeg](img-20.jpeg)

![img-21.jpeg](img-21.jpeg)

![img-22.jpeg](img-22.jpeg)

Page:224
UNIT-12
PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

## 13.1 Construction of a Triangle

### 13.1.1 Construct a triangle having given:

- Two sides and included angles.
- One side and two angles.
- Two of its sides and the angle opposite to one of them. With all three possibilities.

When two sides and the included angle are given.

**Example** Construct a triangle ABC in which $m\overline{AB} = 4.8\mathrm{cm}$, $m\overline{AC} = 3.2\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\angle B = 75^{\circ}$

**Construction:**

i) Draw the line segment $\overline{AB}$ of measure $4.8\mathrm{cm}$.
ii) At point A, draw on angle XAB of measure $75^{\circ}$.
iii) Cut $\overline{AC}$ of measure $3.2\mathrm{cm}$ from $\overline{AX}$
iv) Draw $\overline{BC}$

Thus $\Delta ABC$ is the required triangle.

![img-23.jpeg](img-23.jpeg)

When one side and two angles are given.

**Example 01** Construct a triangle $\Delta ABC$ in which $m\overline{AB} = 5.5\mathrm{cm}$, $m\angle A = 75^{\circ}$ and $m\angle B = 40^{\circ}$

**Construction:**

i) Draw the line segment $\overline{AB}$ of measure $5.5\mathrm{cm}$.
ii) At point A, draw on angle XAB of measure $75^{\circ}$.
iii) At point B draw an angle YBA of measure $40^{\circ}$, such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BY}}$ cuts $\overrightarrow{AX}$ at point C.

Thus $\Delta ABC$ is the required triangle.

![img-24.jpeg](img-24.jpeg)

UNIT-13

PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

225

Example 02 Construct a triangle $\Delta XYZ$ in which

$$
m\angle A = 65^{\circ},\ m\angle B = 40^{\circ} \text{ and } m\overline{BC} = 5.8\ \mathrm{cm}.
$$

**Construction:**

We know that in $\Delta ABC$

$$
m\angle A + m\angle B + m\angle B = 180^{\circ}
$$

Here $m\angle A = 65^{\circ}$ and $m\angle B = 40^{\circ}$

So, $m\angle C = 180^{\circ} - (m\angle A + m\angle B)$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
= 180^{\circ} - (65^{\circ} + 40^{\circ}) \\
= 180^{\circ} - 105^{\circ} \\
= 75^{\circ} \\
\end{array}
$$

We now construct the triangle with

$$
m\overline{BC} = 5.8\ \mathrm{cm},\ m\angle B = 40^{\circ} \text{ and } m\angle C = 75^{\circ}
$$

i) Draw $\overline{BC}$ of measure $5.8\ \mathrm{cm}$.
ii) Draw an angle $XBC = 40^{\circ}$, at point B.
iii) Draw $m\angle Y CB = 75^{\circ}$ at point C.
iv) Rays $\overrightarrow{BX}$ and $\overrightarrow{CY}$ intersect each other at point A,

Thus $\Delta ABC$ is the required triangle.

Two of its sides and the angle opposite to one of them. With all three possibilities.

**Case I**

**Example 01** Construct a triangle $ABC$ in which

$$
m\angle A = 40^{\circ},\ m\overline{BC} = 3.7\ \mathrm{cm} \text{ and } m\overline{AB} = 5.3\ \mathrm{cm}
$$

**Construction:**

i) Draw $\overline{AB}$ of measure $5.3\ \mathrm{cm}$.
ii) At point A, draw $\angle BAX$ of measure $40^{\circ}$.
iii) With center B and radius $3.7\ \mathrm{cm}$, draw an arc which cuts $\overrightarrow{AX}$ at point C and $C'$.
iv) Draw $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{BC'}$.

$\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta ABC'$ are the required triangle.

![img-25.jpeg](img-25.jpeg)

![img-26.jpeg](img-26.jpeg)

![img-27.jpeg](img-27.jpeg)

![img-28.jpeg](img-28.jpeg)

![img-29.jpeg](img-29.jpeg)

![img-30.jpeg](img-30.jpeg)

![img-31.jpeg](img-31.jpeg)

226
UNIT-11
PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

# Case II

**Example 02** Construct a triangle DEF when

$$
m \overline{\mathrm{DE}} = 5.6 \mathrm{~cm}, m \overline{\mathrm{DF}} = 2.5 \mathrm{~cm} \text{ and } m \angle \mathrm{E} = 30^\circ
$$

## Construction:

i) Draw a line segment $\overline{\mathrm{DE}}$ of measure $5.6\mathrm{cm}$.
ii) Draw and angle DEX of measure $30^{\circ}$ at point E.
iii) With D as a center draw an arc of radius $2.5\mathrm{cm}$, which does not cut $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{EX}}$ at any point. In this case no triangle can be constructed satisfying the given data.

![img-32.jpeg](img-32.jpeg)

# Case III

**Example 03** Construct a triangle PQR when

$$
m \overline{\mathrm{PR}} = m \overline{\mathrm{QR}} = 4.7 \mathrm{~cm} \text{ and } m \angle \mathrm{P} = 55^\circ
$$

## Construction:

i) Draw a line segment $\overline{\mathrm{PR}}$ of measure 4.7 cm.
ii) Draw an angle $\angle XPR$ of measure $55^\circ$ at point P.
iii) With point R as a center draw an arc of radius $4.7\mathrm{cm}$, which cuts $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PX}}$ at point Q.
iv) Join point Q and R. $\Delta PQR$ is the required triangle.

![img-33.jpeg](img-33.jpeg)

**Note:** The above case I, case II and case III are called ambiguous cases

nodes.title: Practical Geometry: Triangle Constructions, Properties, and Area Equivalency
nodes.summary: This text provides comprehensive instructions for the practical construction of triangles based on specific side and angle parameters. It details the geometric methods for drawing essential triangle components—angle bisectors, altitudes, perpendicular bisectors, and medians—while noting properties like the concurrency of medians and their centroidal ratio. Additionally, it includes exercises for verifying these properties and outlines geometric techniques for constructing equivalent area figures, specifically transforming quadrilaterals into triangles, triangles into rectangles, and rectangles into squares.
nodes.text:
# Exercise 13.1

1. Construct $\Delta PQR$ such that, $m\overline{PQ} = m\overline{QR} = 4.6$ cm and $m\angle Q = 35^\circ$
2. Construct $\Delta ABC$ such that, $m\overline{AB} = m\overline{AC} = 5.1$ cm and $m\angle A = 65^\circ$
3. Construct $\Delta LMN$ such that, $m\overline{LM} = 3.7$ cm, $m\overline{MN} = 2.5$ cm and $\angle M = 50^\circ$
4. Construct $\Delta ABC$ such that, $m\overline{AB} = 3.5$ cm, $m\overline{BC} = 2.7$ cm and $\angle B = 110^\circ$
5. Construct $\Delta XYZ$ such that, $m\overline{XY} = 4.1$ cm, $m\overline{YZ} = 5$ cm and $\angle Z = 80^\circ$

UNIT-13

PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

UNIT-13

PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

6. Construct the $\Delta$DEF, $\Delta$LMN and $\Delta$ABC in the following.

(i) $m\overline{\mathrm{DE}} = 5\mathrm{cm}, m\angle \mathrm{D} = 45^{\circ}$ and, $m\angle \mathrm{E} = 60^{\circ}$
(ii) $m\overline{\mathrm{LM}} = 6\mathrm{cm}, m\angle \mathrm{L} = 75^{\circ}$ and, $m\angle \mathrm{M} = 45^{\circ}$
(iii) $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = 5.8\mathrm{cm}, m\angle \mathrm{A} = 30^{\circ}$ and, $m\angle \mathrm{B} = 45^{\circ}$

7. Construct a $\Delta$ABC, when lengths of two of its sides and measure of an angle opposite one of the side is given as under:

(i) $m\overline{\mathrm{AC}} = 4.5\mathrm{cm}, m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = 4.1\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\angle \mathrm{B} = 75^{\circ}$
(ii) $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = 5\mathrm{cm}, m\overline{\mathrm{AB}} = 5.5\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\angle \mathrm{C} = 70^{\circ}$
(iii) $m\mathrm{AB} = 5\mathrm{cm}, m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = 5.5\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\angle \mathrm{A} = 45^{\circ}$

## 13.1.2 Draw

- Angle bisectors
- Altitudes
- Perpendicular bisectors
- Medians

## (i) Draw the angle bisector of a given triangle

**Example** Draw bisectors of angle of $\Delta ABC.

**Given:**

ABC is a triangle $\angle A, \angle B$ and $\angle C$ are its angles.

**Required:**

To draw bisectors of $\angle A, \angle B$ and $\angle C$.

**Construction:**

i) Draw the triangle ABC.
ii) With point B as a center draw an arc of any radius, intersecting the sides $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{BA}}$ at points L and M.
iii) Take point L as a center and draw an arc of any radius.
iv) Now take point M as a center and with the same radius draw another arc, which cuts the pervious arc at point P.
v) Join point P to B and produce it.

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BP}}$ is the bisector of $\angle B$.

vi) Repeat steps (ii) to (v) to draw $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CQ}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AR}}$ the bisectors of $\angle C$ and $\angle A$ respectively.

Hence, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BP}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{CQ}}$, and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AR}}$ are the required bisector of $\Delta ABC$.

![img-34.jpeg](img-34.jpeg)

#

#

#

UNIT-13

PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

# (ii) Draw the altitudes of a given triangle

Example Take any triangle ABC and draw its altitudes.

Given:

A  $\Delta ABC$

Required:

To draw altitudes of the  $\Delta ABC$ .

# Construction:

i) Draw the triangle ABC.
ii) Take point A as center and draw an arc of suitable radius, which cuts  $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$  at points D and E.
iii) From D as center, draw an arc of radius more than  $\frac{1}{2} m\overline{\mathrm{DE}}$ .
iv) Again from point E draw another arc of same radius, cutting first arc at point F.
v) Join the points A and F. Such that  $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AF}}$  intersects  $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$  at point P. Then  $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AP}}$  is the altitude of the  $\Delta ABC$  from the vertex A.
vi) Repeat the steps (ii) to (v) and draw  $\overline{\mathrm{BQ}}$  and  $\overline{\mathrm{CR}}$ , the altitudes of  $\Delta ABC$  from the vertices B and C, respectively.

Hence  $\overline{\mathrm{AP}},\overline{\mathrm{BQ}}$  and  $\overline{\mathrm{CR}}$  are required altitudes of  $\Delta ABC$

# (iii) Draw the perpendicular bisector of a given triangle

Example Draw the perpendicular bisector of sides of a triangle ABC.

Given:

A triangle ABC.

Required:

To draw perpendicular bisectors of the sides  $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$ ,  $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$  and  $\overline{\mathrm{CA}}$ .

# Construction:

i) Draw the triangle ABC.
ii) To draw perpendicular bisector of the

![img-35.jpeg](img-35.jpeg)

![img-36.jpeg](img-36.jpeg)

UNIT-13

PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

229

side $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$, with B as a center and radius more than half of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$, draw arcs on either sides of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$.

iii) Now with A as a center and with the same radius, draw arcs on either sides of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$, cutting previous arcs at P and Q.

iv) Join P and Q.

$\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}$ is the perpendicular bisector of the $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$.

v) Repeat the steps (ii) to (iv) and draw $\overline{\mathrm{ST}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{LM}}$, the perpendicular bisectors of $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{AC}}$, respectively.

Hence $\overline{\mathrm{PQ}}, \overline{\mathrm{ST}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{LM}}$ are the required perpendicular bisector of the sides $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}, \overline{\mathrm{BC}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{AC}}$, respectively, of the $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$.

## (iv) Draw the median of a given triangle

**Example** Take any triangle ABC and draw medians of this triangle.

**Given:**

A $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$

**Required:**

To draw medians of the $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$.

**Construction:**

i) Draw the triangle ABC.

ii) Bisect the sides $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$, $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{AC}}$ at points D, E and F, respectively.

iii) Join A to E; B to F and C to D.

Thus $\overline{\mathrm{AE}}$, $\overline{\mathrm{BF}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$ are the required medians of the $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$, which meet in a point G.

It may be noted that medians of every triangle are concurrent (i.e., meet in one point) and their point of concurrency, called centroid, divides each of them in $2:1$.

By actual measurement it can be proved that

$$
\frac {m \overline {{\mathrm {A G}}}}{m \overline {{\mathrm {G E}}}} = \frac {m \overline {{\mathrm {B G}}}}{m \overline {{\mathrm {G F}}}} = \frac {m \overline {{\mathrm {C G}}}}{m \overline {{\mathrm {G D}}}} = \frac {2}{1}
$$

![img-37.jpeg](img-37.jpeg)

230
UNIT-13
PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

# Exercise 13.2

1. Take a $\Delta$ and draw the medians and prove that they are concurrent.
2. Take a $\Delta$ and draw the altitudes and prove that they are concurrent.
3. Take a $\Delta$ and draw the internal bisectors of angles and prove that they are concurrent.
4. Construct a triangle ABC in which $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = 6$ cm, $m\overline{\mathrm{CA}} = 4$ cm and $m\overline{\mathrm{AB}} = 5$ cm, draw the bisectors of angles A and B.
5. Construct a triangle PQR in which $m\overline{\mathrm{PQ}} = 5.7\mathrm{cm}$, $m\overline{\mathrm{QR}} = 6.4\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\overline{\mathrm{PR}} = 4.4\mathrm{cm}$, draw the altitudes from vertex R and vertex Q.
6. Construct a triangle STU in which $\angle T = 60^{\circ}$, $\angle U = 30^{\circ}$ and $m\overline{\mathrm{TU}} = 7$ cm. Find the perpendicular bisectors of the sides of triangle and prove that they are concurrent.
7. Construct a right triangle ABC in which $\angle C = 90^{\circ}$, $\angle B = 45^{\circ}$ and $m\overline{\mathrm{CB}} = 5$ cm. Draw the medians of the triangle.
8. Construct the following $\Delta XYZ$. Draw their three medians and show that they are concurrent.
(i) $m\overline{YZ} = 4.4\mathrm{cm}, m\angle Y = 45^{\circ}$ and $m\angle Z = 75^{\circ}$
(ii) $m\overline{XY} = 4.6\mathrm{cm}, m m\overline{XZ} = 4.6\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\angle Y = 60^{\circ}$
9. Construct the $\Delta$KLM, in which $m\overline{\mathrm{KL}} = 4.8\mathrm{cm}$, $m\overline{\mathrm{LM}} = 5.2\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\overline{\mathrm{MK}} = 4.5\mathrm{cm}$, draw their altitudes and verify their concurrency.
10. Construct the $\Delta$PQR, in which $m\overline{\mathrm{PQ}} = 7\mathrm{cm}$, $m\overline{\mathrm{QR}} = 6.5\mathrm{cm}$ and $m\overline{\mathrm{PR}} = 5.8\mathrm{cm}$, find their perpendicular bisectors and verify their concurrency.

## 13.2 Figures with equal Areas

### 13.2.1 Construct a triangle equal in area to a given quadrilateral.

E.g. draw a triangle equal in area to given quadrilateral ABCD. We know that, Area of all triangles with same base equal of vertices are on the line perpendicular to base.

1. ABCD is a given quadrilateral.
2. Join A to C.
4. Through D, draw $\overline{\mathrm{DE}}$ parallel to $\overline{\mathrm{AC}}$ meeting $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$ produced at point E.
5. Join A to E, then ABE is the required triangle.

![img-38.jpeg](img-38.jpeg)

nodes.title: Practical Geometric Constructions and Area Theorems
nodes.summary: This text covers practical geometric construction techniques, including transforming triangles into rectangles, rectangles into squares, and triangles into equivalent triangles with specific base lengths. It provides exercises for applying these constructions, a review section testing knowledge of triangle properties and concurrency, a summary of construction concepts, and an introduction to theorems concerning the areas of parallelograms and triangles.

PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

231

## 13.2.2 Construct a rectangle equal in area to a given triangle.

E.g. Construct a rectangle equal in area to given $\Delta ABC$

1. Draw a triangle $ABC$.
2. Draw a perpendicular bisector $\overline{\mathrm{PD}}$ of $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$.
3. Through A, draw a line PQ parallel to $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$.
4. Take $m\overline{\mathrm{PQ}} = m\overline{\mathrm{DC}}$.
5. Then CDPQ is the required rectangle.

![img-39.jpeg](img-39.jpeg)

## 13.2.3 Construct a Square equal in area to a given rectangle.

E.g. Construct a square equal in Area to given rectangle ABCD.

Following are the steps of construction

1. ABCD is a given rectangle.
2. Produce side $\overline{\mathrm{AD}}$ to E making $m\overline{\mathrm{DE}} = m\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$.
3. Bisect $\overline{\mathrm{AE}}$ at M.
4. With centre M and radius $m\overline{\mathrm{AM}}$ construct a semi circle.
5. Produce $\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$ to meet the semi circle at F.
6. On $\overline{\mathrm{DF}}$ as a side construct a square DFQP. This shall be required square.

![img-40.jpeg](img-40.jpeg)

232
UNIT:12
PRACTICAL GEOMETRY - TRIANGLES

## 13.2.4 Construct a Triangle of equivalent area on a base of given length:

Following are the steps of construction

1. ABC is given triangle.
2. Draw $\overleftrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}} \parallel \overleftrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}$.
3. With B as centre, and radius = x, such that $m\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}} = x$ draw an arc cutting $\overleftrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}$ at P.
4. Join $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BP}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CP}}$.
5. Then $\Delta \mathrm{BCP}$ is the required triangle with equal base $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BP}} = x$ and area equivalent to $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$.

![img-41.jpeg](img-41.jpeg)

## Exercise 13.3

1. Construct a rectangle whose adjacent sides are $2.5\mathrm{cm}$ and $5\mathrm{cm}$ respectively. Construct a square having area equal to the given rectangle.
2. Construct a square equal in area to a rectangle whose adjacent sides are $4.5\mathrm{cm}$ and $2.2\mathrm{cm}$ respectively. Measure the sides of the square and find its area and compare with the area of the rectangle. Also verify by measurement that the perimeter of the square is less than that of the rectangle.
3. Construct a triangle having base $4\mathrm{cm}$ and other two sides equal to $3.6\mathrm{cm}$ and $3.8\mathrm{cm}$ each. Transform it into a rectangle with equal Area.
4. Construct a triangle having base 6cm and other sides equal to 5cm and 6cm each. Construct a rectangle equal in area to given $\Delta$.

# Summary

- In this unit we have learnt the construction of the following figures and relevant concepts.
- To construct a triangle, having given two sides and the included angle.
- To construct a triangle, having given one side and two of the angles.
- To construct a triangle, having given two of its sides and the angle opposite to one of them.
- To draw angle bisectors of a given triangle and to verify their concurrency.
- To draw altitudes of a given triangle and verify their concurrency.
- To draw perpendicular bisectors of the sides of a given triangle and to verify their concurrency.
- To draw medians of a given triangle and verify their concurrency.
- To construct a triangle equal in area to a given quadrilateral.
- To construct a rectangle equal in area to given triangle.
- To construct a square equal in area to given rectangle.
- To construct a triangle of equivalent area on the base of given length.
- Two or more than three lines are said to be concurrent if these passes through a common point and that point is called the point of concurrency.
- The point where the internal bisectors of the angles of a triangle intersect is called the in-centre of a triangle.
- The point of concurrency of the perpendicular bisectors of the sides of a triangle is called its circum-centre.
- Median of a triangle means a line segment joining a vertex of a triangle to the mid-point of the opposite side.
- Ortho-centre of a triangle means the point of concurrency of three altitudes of a triangle.

nodes.title: 14.0 Theorems Related with Area
nodes.summary: This text covers Unit 14, focusing on geometric theorems related to the area of parallelograms and triangles. It provides formal proofs, constructions, and logical reasoning for four key theorems: parallelograms on the same or equal bases with equal altitudes are equal in area, and triangles on the same or equal bases with equal altitudes are equal in area. Additionally, it includes corollaries relating parallelograms to rectangles and triangles with common vertices.
nodes.text:
Unit 14

Weightage = 5%

# THEOREMS RELATED WITH AREA

## Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs)

After completing this unit, students will be able to:

- Understand the following theorems along with their corollaries and apply them to solve allied problems.
- Parallelograms on the same base and lying between the same parallel lines (or of the same altitude) are equal in area.
- Parallelograms on equal bases and having the same altitude are equal in area.
- The triangles on the same base and of the same altitude are equal in area.
- Triangles on equal bases and of the same altitude are equal in area.

# Introduction

We will study the theorems related with Area.

# 14.1 Theorems Related with Area

# Theorem 14.1.1

Parallelograms on the same base and lying between the same parallel lines (or of the same altitude) are equal in area.

# Given:

Two parallelograms ABCD and ABEF with the same base  $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$  and between the same parallels segments  $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$  and  $\overline{\mathrm{DE}}$ .

![img-47.jpeg](img-47.jpeg)

# To prove:

Parallelograms ABCD and ABEF are equal in areas, i.e.  $\blacksquare$  ABCD = ABEF.

# Proof:

|  Statements |   | Reasons  |   |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  In ΔBCE ↔ ΔADF |   |   |   |
|  (i) | mBC = mAD | ... (i) | (i) Opposite sides of ||m ABCD are equal.  |
|  (ii) | m∠BCE = m ∠ADF | ... (ii) | (ii) Corresponding angles of ||m ABCD.  |
|  (iii) | ∠E ≈ ∠F | ... (iii) | (iii) Corresponding angles of ||m ABEF.  |
|  ∴ ΔBCE ≈ ΔADF |   | S.A.A ≈ S.A.A  |   |
|  ∴ ▲BCE ≈ ▲ADF |   | Congruent figures are equal in area.  |   |
|  ABED + ▲BCE = ABED + ▲ADF |   | Adding same area on both sides  |   |
|  Thus, ABCD = ABEF. |   | ABCD = ABED + ▲BCE  |   |
|   |  | ABEF = ABED + ▲ADF  |   |

# Q.E.D

# Corollary

(i) The area of parallelogram is equal to that of a rectangle on the same base and having the same altitude.

#

UNIT 14
THEOREMS RELATED WITH AREA
237

# Theorem 14.1.2

Parallelograms on equal bases and having the same altitude are equal in area.

## Given:

Parallelograms ABCD and EFGH are on the equal bases $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{FG}}$, having equal altitudes.

To Prove: $\blacksquare \mathrm{ABCD} = \blacksquare \mathrm{EFGH}$.

## Construction:

Place the parallelograms ABCD and EFGH so that their equal bases $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{FG}}$ are on the same straight line. Join B to E and C to H.

## Proof:

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  $||^m \mathrm{ABCD}$ and $||^m \mathrm{EFGH}$ are between the same parallel segments $\overline{\mathrm{AH}}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{BG}}$. Hence, A, D, E and H are points lying on a straight line parallel to $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$. $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = m\overline{\mathrm{FG}}$ $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = m\overline{\mathrm{EH}}$ $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = m\overline{\mathrm{EH}}$ also these are parallel Hence, EBCH is a parallelogram Now $\blacksquare \mathrm{ABCD} = \blacksquare \mathrm{EBCH} \quad \ldots (\mathrm{i})$ But $\blacksquare \mathrm{EBCH} = \blacksquare \mathrm{EFGH} \quad \ldots (\mathrm{ii})$ Thus, $\blacksquare \mathrm{ABCD} = \blacksquare \mathrm{EFGH}$ | Their altitudes are equal (given) Given $\mathrm{EFGH}$ is a parallelogram and $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = m\overline{\mathrm{FG}}$ Segment of parallel lines are also parallel segments. A quadrilateral with two parallel opposite sides is a parallelogram Theorem 14.1.1 Theorem 14.1.1 From (i) and (ii)  |

Q.E.D

238
UNIT 14
THEOREMS RELATED WITH AREA

# Theorem 14.1.3

Triangles on the same base and of the same altitude are equal in area.

Given:
$\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta DBC$ are on the same base $\overline{BC}$ and between the same parallel lines $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{AD}$.

To prove:
$\triangle ABC = \triangle DBC$

Construction:
Draw $\overline{BE} \parallel \overline{CA}$, meeting at $\overline{AD}$ produced, at E and also draw $\overline{CF} \parallel \overline{BD}$, meeting at $\overline{AD}$ produced at F.

Proof :

![img-48.jpeg](img-48.jpeg)

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  BCAE is a parallelogram. $\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2} \text{ (BCAE)} \quad \ldots \text{ (i)}$ | By construction Diagonal $\overline{AD}$ divides parallelogram BCAE into two $\Delta$s of equal areas.  |
|  Similarly BCFD is a parallelogram $\triangle DBC = \frac{1}{2} \text{ (BCFD)} \quad \ldots \text{ (ii)}$ | By construction Diagonal $\overline{CD}$ divides parallelogram BCFD into two triangles of equal areas.  |
|  BCAE = BCFD $\triangle ABC = \triangle DBC$ | Theorem 14.1.1 From (i),(ii) and (iii)  |

nodes.title: Theorems on Triangle Areas and Geometric Projections
nodes.summary: This text covers the proof and application of theorems concerning the areas of triangles and parallelograms, specifically focusing on figures with equal bases and altitudes. It includes a formal proof, a corollary, and a comprehensive set of exercises and review questions to test understanding of geometric area properties. Additionally, it provides a summary of the unit's key definitions and introduces the learning objectives for the subsequent unit on the projection of triangle sides.
nodes.text:
#Theorem 14.1.4

Triangles on equal bases and of equal altitudes are equal in area.

Given:
$\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta DEF$ are on equal bases $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{EF}$, respectively, and having equal altitudes.

To prove:
$\triangle ABC = \triangle DEF$

Construction:
Draw $\overline{AD}, \overline{BF}$ containing points B, C, E, F.

![img-49.jpeg](img-49.jpeg)

UNIT 14 THEOREMS RELATED WITH AREA 239
![img-50.jpeg](img-50.jpeg)

Place the $\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta DEF$ so that their equal bases $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{EF}$ are on the straight line. Draw $\overline{BX} \parallel \overrightarrow{CA}$ and $\overrightarrow{FY} \parallel \overrightarrow{ED}$. Such that point $X$ and $Y$ lie on $\overrightarrow{AD}$.

**Proof :**

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  $\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta DEF$ are between the same parallel lines. $\overleftrightarrow{BF} \parallel \overrightarrow{XY}$ ∴ $\blacksquare BCAX = \blacksquare EFYD$ ... (i) | Altitudes are equal (given) construction Theorem 14.1.2  |
|  But, $\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2} (\blacksquare BCAX)$ ... (ii) and $\triangle DEF = \frac{1}{2} (\blacksquare EFYD)$ ... (iii) ∴ $\triangle ABC = \triangle DEF$. | Diagonal of a parallelogram divides $\parallel^m$ into two equal triangles By same reason From eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii)  |

**Q.E.D**

**Corollary:** Triangles having a common vertex and equal bases in the same straight line are equal in area.
 UNIT 14 THEOREMS RELATED WITH AREA Page:239
![img-50.jpeg](img-50.jpeg)

Place the $\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta DEF$ so that their equal bases $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{EF}$ are on the straight line. Draw $\overline{BX} \parallel \overrightarrow{CA}$ and $\overrightarrow{FY} \parallel \overrightarrow{ED}$. Such that point $X$ and $Y$ lie on $\overrightarrow{AD}$.

**Proof :**

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  $\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta DEF$ are between the same parallel lines. $\overleftrightarrow{BF} \parallel \overrightarrow{XY}$ ∴ $\blacksquare BCAX = \blacksquare EFYD$ ... (i) | Altitudes are equal (given) construction Theorem 14.1.2  |
|  But, $\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2} (\blacksquare BCAX)$ ... (ii) and $\triangle DEF = \frac{1}{2} (\blacksquare EFYD)$ ... (iii) ∴ $\triangle ABC = \triangle DEF$. | Diagonal of a parallelogram divides $\parallel^m$ into two equal triangles By same reason From eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii)  |

**Q.E.D**

**Corollary:** Triangles having a common vertex and equal bases in the same straight line are equal in area.

ABC is a right-angled at vertex B with  $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = 7\mathrm{cm}$  and  $m\overline{\mathrm{AC}} = 11\mathrm{cm}$  , also  $\Delta$  ABC and  $\Delta$  BCD are on the same base  $\overline{\mathrm{BC}}$  . Find the area of  $\Delta$  BCD.

4. Show that a median of a triangle divides it into two triangles of equal area.
5. Show that the line segment joining the mid-points of the opposite sides of a rectangle, divides it into two equal rectangles.

UNIT 14
THEOREMS RELATED WITH AREA

# Review Exercise 14

1. Mark 'T' for True and 'F' for False in front of each given below:

(i) Area of a closed figure means region enclosed by bounding lines of the figure. T/F
(ii) A diagonal of rectangle divides it into two congruent triangles. T/F
(iii) Congruent figures have different areas. T/F
(iv) The area of parallelogram is equal to the product of base and height. T/F
(v) Median of a triangle means perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side (base). T/F
(vi) Perpendicular distance between two parallel lines can sometimes be different. T/F
(vii) An altitude drawn from a vertex always bisects the opposite side. T/F
(viii) Two triangles are equal in areas, if they have the same base and equal altitude. T/F

2. Tick (✓) the correct answer.

(a) Perpendicular to each other
(b) Parallel to each other
(c) Intersecting to each other
(d) None of these.

(ii) If two triangles have equal areas then they will ___________ be congruent as well.

(a) Not necessarily
(b) Necessarily
(c) Definitely
(d) None of these.

(iii) Perpendicular from a vertex of a triangle to its opposite side is called ___________.

(a) Median
(b) Perpendicular bisector
(c) Altitude
(d) Angle bisector

UNIT 34
THEOREMS RELATED WITH AREA

(iv) Parallelograms having same base and same altitude are _____.
(a) Congruent
(b) Equal in areas
(c) Similar
(d) All of these.

(v) Two parallelograms have equal bases. They will be having the same area, if _____________.
(a) Their altitudes are equal
(b) Their altitude is the same
(c) They lies between the same parallel lines
(d) All of these.

(vi) $\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta DEF$ have equal bases and equal altitudes, then triangles are _____________.
(a) Equal in area
(b) Congruent
(c) Similar
(d) None of these.

**Summary**

- In this unit we have mentioned some necessary preliminaries, stated and proved the following theorems along with corollaries, if any.
- Parallelograms on the same base and between the same parallel lines (or of the same altitude) are equal in areas.
- Parallelograms on the equal bases and having the same (or equal) altitude are equal in areas.
- Triangles on the same base and of the same (i.e. equal) altitudes are equal in areas.
- Triangles on equal bases and of equal altitudes are equal in areas.
- Area of a figure means region enclosed by the boundary lines of a closed figure.
- A triangular region means the union of triangle and its interior.
- By area of triangle means the area of its triangular region.
- Altitude or height of a triangle means perpendicular distance to base from its opposite vertex.

nodes.title: 15.0 Projection of a side of a triangle
nodes.summary: This text covers the geometric properties of triangles, specifically focusing on the projection of sides. It provides formal proofs for theorems concerning the squares of sides opposite to obtuse and acute angles, includes illustrative examples and exercises for practical application, and introduces Apollonius' theorem, which relates the sum of the squares of two sides of a triangle to the square of the median and half of the third side.
nodes.text:
Unit 15

Weightage = 7%

# PROJECTION OF A SIDE OF A TRIANGLE

## Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs)

After completing this unit, students will be able to:

- In an obtuse-angled triangle, the square on the side opposite to the obtuse angle is equal to the sum of the squares on the sides containing the obtuse angle together with twice the rectangle contained by one of the sides, and the projection on it of the other.
- In any triangle, the square on the side opposite to an acute angle is equal to the sum of the squares on the sides containing that acute angle diminished by twice the rectangle contained by one of those sides and the projection on it of the other.
- In any triangle, the sum of the squares on any two sides is equal to twice the square on half of the third side together with twice the square on the median which bisects the third side, (Apollonius' theorem).

![img-54.jpeg](img-54.jpeg)

#

1

#

UNIT-15

PROJECTION OF A SIDE OF A TRIANGLE

# 15.1 Projection of a side of a Triangle

Understand the following theorems along with their corollaries and their applications to solve the allied problems.

# Theorem 15.1.1

In an obtuse-angled triangle, the square on the side opposite to the obtuse angle is equal to the sum of the squares on the sides containing the obtuse angle together with twice the rectangle contained by one of the sides, and the projection on it of the other.

Given:

$\Delta ABC$  with an obtuse angle at vertex A.

To prove:

i.e.  $a^2 = b^2 +c^2 +2cx$

Construction:

Draw perpendicular  $\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$  on  $\overline{\mathrm{BA}}$  produced meeting at point D, so that  $\overline{\mathrm{AD}}$  is the projection of  $\overline{\mathrm{AC}}$  on  $\overline{\mathrm{BA}}$  produced.

![img-55.jpeg](img-55.jpeg)

Taking,  $m\overline{\mathrm{BC}} = a$ ,  $m\overline{\mathrm{CA}} = b$ , and  $m\overline{\mathrm{AB}} = c$  also  $m\overline{\mathrm{AD}} = x$  and  $m\overline{\mathrm{CD}} = h$ .

Proof:

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  In right angled ΔCDA m∠CDA = 90° so, (mAC)2 = (mAD)2 + (mDC)2 i.e. b2 = x2 + h2 ... (i), In right angled ΔCDB m∠CDA = 90° so, (mBC)2 = (mBD)2 + (mCD)2 i.e. a2 = (c + x)2 + h2 or a2 = c2 + 2cx + x2 + h2 ... (ii) a2 = c2 + 2cx + b2 Thus (mBC)2 = (mAB)2 + 2(mAB)(mAD) + (mAC)2 | Construction By Pythagoras theorem. By supposition Construction By Pythagoras theorem. mBD = mBA + mAD b2 = x2 + h2  |

Q.E.D

UNIT-15
PROJECTION OF A SIDE OF A TRIANGLE
245

Example: In a $\Delta ABC$ with obtuse angle is at vertex A, if $\overline{CD}$ is an altitude on $\overline{BA}$ produced, meeting at point D, and $\mathsf{mAC} = \mathsf{mAB}$

Then, $\left(m\overline{BC}\right)^2 = 2\left(m\overline{AB}\right)\left(m\overline{BD}\right)$.

Given:

In a $\Delta ABC$, $m\angle A$ is an obtuse, $m\overline{AC} = m\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$ being altitude on $\overline{BA}$ produced, meeting at point D.

To prove:

$$
\left(m \overline{BC}\right) ^ {2} = 2 \left(m \overline{AB}\right) \left(m \overline{BD}\right).
$$

Proof:

![img-56.jpeg](img-56.jpeg)

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  In a $\Delta ABC$ |   |
|  $\left(m\overline{BC}\right)^2 = \left(m\overline{AB}\right)^2 + 2\left(m\overline{AB}\right)\left(m\overline{AD}\right) + \left(m\overline{AC}\right)^2$ | Theorem 15.1.1  |
|  $\left(m\overline{BC}\right)^2 = \left(m\overline{AB}\right)^2 + 2\left(m\overline{AB}\right)\left(m\overline{AD}\right) + \left(m\overline{AB}\right)^2$ | Given that $m\overline{AC} = m\overline{AB}$  |
|  $\left(m\overline{BC}\right)^2 = 2\left(m\overline{AB}\right)^2 + 2\left(m\overline{AB}\right)\left(m\overline{AD}\right)$ | Taking $2m\overline{AB}$ as common  |
|  $\left(m\overline{BC}\right)^2 = 2m\overline{AB}\left(m\overline{AB} + m\overline{AD}\right)$ | $m\overline{BD} = m\overline{AD} + m\overline{AB}$  |
|  $\left(m\overline{BC}\right)^2 = 2\left(m\overline{AB}\right)\left(m\overline{BD}\right)$ |   |

Q.E.D

246
UNIT-15
PROJECTION OF A SIDE OF A TRIANGLE

# Exercise 15.1

1. Find the length of $\overline{AB}$ and area of the triangle $ABC$, when

(i) $m\overline{AC} = 3cm$, $m\overline{BC} = 6cm$ and $m\angle C = 120^\circ$, where

$$
m\overline{CD} = m\overline{BC} \cos(180^\circ - m\angle C)
$$

(ii) $m\overline{AC} = 40mm$, $m\overline{BC} = 80mm$ and $m\angle C = 120^\circ$, where

$$
m\overline{CD} = m\overline{BC} \cos(180^\circ - m\angle C)
$$

Hint: $(m\overline{BC})^2 = (m\overline{AC})^2 + (m\overline{AB})^2 + 2(m\overline{AB})(m\overline{AD})$

2. Find the length of $m\overline{AC}$ in the $\Delta ABC$ when $m\overline{BC} = 6cm$, $m\overline{AB} = 4\sqrt{2}cm$ and $m\angle ABC = 135^\circ$. If possible, find the area of the $\Delta ABC$.

3. Find the length of $m\overline{AC}$ in the $\Delta ABC$ when $m\overline{BC} = 6\sqrt{2}cm$, $m\overline{AB} = 8cm$ and $m\angle ABC = 135^\circ$. If possible, find the area of the $\Delta ABC$.

# Theorem 15.1.2

In any triangle, the square on the side opposite to an acute angle is equal to sum of the squares on the sides containing that acute angle diminished by twice the rectangle contained by one of those sides and the projection on it of the other.

**Given:**

$\Delta ABC$ with an acute $\angle CAB$ at vertex $A$.

Taking, $m\overline{BC} = a$, $m\overline{AC} = b$ and $m\overline{AB} = c$

**Construction:**

Draw $\overline{CD} \perp \overline{AB}$ so that $\overline{AD}$ is projection of $\overline{AC}$ on $\overline{AB}$

Also $m\overline{AD} = x$ and $m\overline{CD} = h$

**To prove:**

$$
(m\overline{BC})^2 = (m\overline{AC})^2 + (m\overline{AB})^2 - 2(m\overline{AB})(m\overline{AD})
$$

i.e. $a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2cx$

![img-57.jpeg](img-57.jpeg)

nodes.title: Projections, Apollonius' Theorem, and Introduction to Coordinate Geometry
nodes.summary: This text covers the geometric properties of triangles, specifically focusing on the projection of sides, the proof of Apollonius' theorem, and practical applications through exercises and review problems. It provides formal proofs for side relationships in triangles and concludes with a summary of key theorems. Additionally, it introduces Unit 16, outlining learning objectives for coordinate geometry, including the use of distance and midpoint formulas to classify geometric shapes.

UNIT-15
PROJECTION OF A SIDE OF A TRIANGLE
247

## Proof:

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  In right angled ΔCDA, $m \angle CDA = 90^\circ$, so, $\left( m\overline{AC} \right)^2 = \left( m\overline{AD} \right)^2 + \left( m\overline{CD} \right)^2$ i.e. $b^2 = x^2 + h^2$ ... (i) | Construction Using Pythagoras Theorem. By supposition  |
|  In right angled ΔCDA $m \angle CDB = 90^\circ$ $\left( m\overline{BC} \right)^2 = \left( m\overline{BD} \right)^2 + \left( m\overline{CD} \right)^2$ | Construction By Pythagoras Theorem.  |
|  so, i.e. $a^2 = (c - x)^2 + h^2$ $a^2 = c^2 - 2cx + x^2 + h^2$ or $a^2 = c^2 - 2cx + b^2$ ... (ii) $a^2 = c^2 + b^2 - 2cx$ | From the figure. $\therefore m\overline{BD} = m\overline{AB} - m\overline{AD}$  |
|  Hence $\left( m\overline{BC} \right)^2 = \left( m\overline{AC} \right)^2 + \left( m\overline{AB} \right)^2 - 2\left( m\overline{AB} \right)\left( m\overline{AD} \right)$ | Using equation... (i)  |

## Q.E.D

## Apollonius and the theorem of Apollonius:

Apollonius was a great geometer and astronomer.

Now we state and prove one of his well-known theorem "the Apollonius theorem".

## Theorem 15.1.3 (Apollonius theorem)

In any triangle, the sum of the squares on any two sides is equal to twice the square on half of the third side together with twice the square on the median which bisects the third side, (Apollonius' theorem).

## Given:

In $\Delta ABC$, the median $\overline{AD}$ bisects $\overline{BC}$ at point D.

such that $m\overline{BD} = m\overline{CD}$.

![img-58.jpeg](img-58.jpeg)
![img-59.jpeg](img-59.jpeg)

248

To prove:
$$
\left(m \overline{A} \overline{B}\right)^2 + \left(m \overline{A} \overline{C}\right)^2 = 2 \left(m \overline{B} \overline{D}\right)^2 + 2 \left(m \overline{A} \overline{D}\right)^2
$$

Construction:
Draw $\overline{AF} \perp \overline{BC}$.

Proof:

|  Statements | Reasons  |
| --- | --- |
|  In $\Delta ADB$ Since, $\angle ADB$ is acute | $\Delta ADF$ is right angled triangle with right angled at F (construction)  |
|  So, $\left(m \overline{A} \overline{B}\right)^2 = \left(m \overline{B} \overline{D}\right)^2 + \left(m \overline{A} \overline{D}\right)^2 - 2 \left(m \overline{B} \overline{D}\right) \left(m \overline{F} \overline{D}\right) \dots (\mathrm{i})$ | Using theorem 15.1.2  |
|  Now, In $\Delta ADC$ We $\angle ADC$ is an obtuse angle at point D. | Supplement of in $\angle ADB$  |
|  So, $\left(m \overline{A} \overline{C}\right)^2 = \left(m \overline{C} \overline{D}\right)^2 + \left(m \overline{A} \overline{D}\right)^2 + 2 \left(m \overline{C} \overline{D}\right) \left(m \overline{F} \overline{D}\right)$ | Theorem 15.1.1  |
|  $\left(m \overline{A} \overline{C}\right)^2 = \left(m \overline{B} \overline{D}\right)^2 + \left(m \overline{A} \overline{D}\right)^2 + 2 \left(m \overline{B} \overline{D}\right) \left(m \overline{F} \overline{D}\right) \dots (\mathrm{ii})$ | $m \overline{C} \overline{D} = m \overline{B} \overline{D}$  |
|  Thus, $\left(m \overline{A} \overline{B}\right)^2 + \left(m \overline{A} \overline{C}\right)^2 = 2 \left(m \overline{B} \overline{D}\right)^2 + 2 \left(m \overline{A} \overline{D}\right)^2$ | Adding eqs. (i) and (ii)  |

PROJECTION OF A SIDE OF A TRIANGLE

## Summary

- In an obtuse-angled triangle, the square on the side opposite to the obtuse angle is equal to the sum of the squares on the sides containing the obtuse angle together with twice the rectangle contained by one of the sides, and the projection on it of the other.
- In any triangle, the square on the side opposite to an acute angle is equal to the sum of the squares on the sides containing that acute angle diminished by twice the rectangle contained by one of those sides and the projection on it of the other.
- In any triangle, the sum of the squares on any two sides is equal to twice the square on half the third side together with twice the square on the median which bisects the third side, (Apollonius theorem).

nodes.title: 16.0 Introduction To  Coordinate Geometry /Analytical Geometry
nodes.summary: This text introduces coordinate geometry, explaining its role in linking algebra and geometry. It provides the formal derivation of the distance formula using the Pythagorean theorem and demonstrates its application through various examples, including calculating distances between points and verifying positions relative to a circle. Additionally, it defines collinear and non-collinear points, establishing the distance-based criteria required to identify if three or more points lie on the same line.
nodes.text:
Unit 16

Weightage = 5%

# Introduction To Coordinate Geometry / Analytical Geometry

## Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs)

After completing this unit, students will be able to:

- Explain and define coordinate geometry.
- Derive distance formula to calculate distance between two points given in Cartesian plane.
- Use distance formula to find distance between two given points.
- Define collinear points. Distinguish between collinear and non-collinear points.
- Use distance formula to show that three (or more) given points are collinear.
- Use distance formula to show that the given three non-collinear points form:
- i. An equilateral triangle,
- ii. An isosceles triangle,
- iii. A right angled triangle,
- iv. A scalene triangle.
- Use distance formula to show that four given non-collinear points form:
- A square,
- A rectangle,
- A parallelogram.
- Recognize the formula to find the midpoint of the line joining two given points.
- Apply distance and midpoint formulas to solve/verify different standard results related to geometry.

252
UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY

# Introduction

The Cartesian coordinate was invented in the 17th century by Rene Descartes (Latinized name as Cartesius) revolutionized mathematics by providing the first systematic link between Euclidean geometry and algebra. Using the Cartesian co-ordinate system, geometric shapes (such as lines and curves) can be described by equations.

## 16.1 Distance Formula

### 16.1.1 Explain and define Coordinate Geometry

Co-ordinate geometry is one of the most important and exciting branch of mathematics. In particular it is central to the mathematics students meet at school. It provides a connection between algebra and geometry through graphs of lines and curves.

The algebraic study of geometry with the help of coordinate system is called co-ordinate geometry/analytical geometry.

This enables geometrical problems to be solved algebraically and provides geometric insights into algebra. It is a part of geometry in which ordered pairs of numbers are used to describe the position of a point on a plane. Here, the concept of coordinate geometry (also known as Cartesian geometry) and its formulas and their derivations will be explained.

### 16.1.2 Derive distance formula to calculate the distance between two given points in the Cartesian plane

**Statement:**

The distance between any two points  $\mathrm{P}_1(x_1,y_1)$  and  $\mathrm{P}_2(x_2,y_2)$  is denoted as  $|\overline{\mathrm{P}_1\mathrm{P}_2}|$  and is defined as:

$$
\left| \overline {{\mathrm {P} _ {1} \mathrm {P} _ {2}}} \right| = \sqrt {\left(x _ {2} - x _ {1}\right) ^ {2} + \left(y _ {2} - y _ {1}\right) ^ {2}}
$$

**Derivation of the Distance Formula**

Let  $\mathrm{P}_1(x_1,y_1)$  and  $\mathrm{P}_2(x_2,y_2)$  be the any two points in plane.

![img-60.jpeg](img-120.jpeg)

UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY
253

From $\mathrm{P}_1$ and $\mathrm{P}_2$ draw perpendiculars $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_1\mathrm{A}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{A}}$ on $x$-axis, Also draw a $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_1\mathrm{C}}$ parallel to $x$-axes.

$$
\left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {P} _ {1} \mathrm {C}} \right| = \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {A B}} \right| = \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {O B}} \right| - \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {O A}} \right| = \left| x _ {2} - x _ {1} \right|
$$

and $\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{C}}\right| = \left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{B}}\right| - \left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}\right| = \left|y_2 - y_1\right|$

Consider right angled $\Delta P_1 C P_2$ and Appling Pythagoras theorem, we have,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {P} _ {1} \mathrm {P} _ {2}} \right| ^ {2} = \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {P} _ {1} \mathrm {C}} \right| ^ {2} + \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {P} _ {2} \mathrm {C}} \right| ^ {2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {P} _ {1} \mathrm {P} _ {2}} \right| ^ {2} = \left(x _ {2} - x _ {1}\right) ^ {2} + \left(y _ {2} - y _ {2}\right) ^ {2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \left| \overrightarrow {\mathrm {P} _ {1} \mathrm {P} _ {2}} \right| = \sqrt {\left(x _ {2} - x _ {1}\right) ^ {2} + \left(y _ {2} - y _ {2}\right) ^ {2}} \\
\end{array}
$$

**Note:**

The distance $d$ from origin to the point $P(x, y)$ is:

$$
d = \sqrt {x ^ {2} + y ^ {2}}
$$

## 16.1.3 Use Distance Formula to find the distance between two given points.

The following examples will help to understand the use of distance formula.

**Example 01** Find the distance between the point $\mathrm{P}(2,3)$ and $\mathrm{Q}(-4,5)$

**Solution:** By using distance formula $\left|\mathrm{PQ}\right| = \sqrt{\left(x_2 - x_1\right)^2 + \left(y_2 - y_1\right)^2}$

Here

$$
\begin{array}{l}
(x _ {1}, y _ {1}) = (2, 3), (x _ {2}, y _ {2}) = (- 4, 5) \\
\therefore \quad \left| \mathrm {P Q} \right| = \sqrt {\left(- 4 - 2\right) ^ {2} + \left(5 - 3\right) ^ {2}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \left| \mathrm {P Q} \right| = \sqrt {\left(- 6\right) ^ {2} + \left(2\right) ^ {2}} = \sqrt {3 6 + 4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \left| \mathrm {P Q} \right| = \sqrt {4 0} = 2 \sqrt {1 0} \\
\end{array
$$

254
UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY

Example 02 Circle with radius 5 unit is drawn with centre $C(3,2)$ and $L(6,6), M(0,-1)$ and $N(-2,-3)$ points are given. Find which of the point is not on the circle. (give reason).

Solution:

$C(3,2)$ is the centre of a circle with radius 5 units.

and $L(6,6), M(0,-1), N(-2,-3)$ are three given points.

We know that,

We have to, find the length (distance) from $C$ to $L$, $M$ and $N$ respectively using distance formula, then,

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad \left| \overline{CL} \right| = \sqrt{(6 - 3)^2 + (6 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ units}, \\
\left| \overline{CM} \right| = \sqrt{(0 - 3)^2 + (-1 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} = 3\sqrt{2} \text{ units}, \\
\left| \overline{CN} \right| = \sqrt{(-2 - 3)^2 + (-3 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} = 5\sqrt{2} \text{ units}, \\
\left| \overline{CL} \right| = 5 \text{ units i.e., radius of the circle} \\
\left| \overline{CM} \right| = 3\sqrt{2} \text{ units} < 5 \text{ units} \\
\left| \overline{CN} \right| = 5\sqrt{2} \text{ units} > 5 \text{ units}
\end{array}
$$

So, and $N(-2,-3)$ are not on the circle.

Exercise 16.1

1. Using distance formula find the distance between the following pairs of points.

(i) $(-4,5)$ and $(6,6)$

(ii) $(2,2)$ and $(2,3)$

(iii) $(0,1)$ and $(2,3)$

(iv) $(0,1)$ and $(2,3)$

2. $A(a,0)$ and $B(0,b)$ be the points on the axes, find the distance between A and B, when

(i) $a = -3, b = -4$

(ii) $a = -9, b = 6$

(iii) $a = 3, b = 4$

(iv) $a = \sqrt{2}, b = -2\sqrt{2}$

3. Find the perimeter of the triangle formed by the points.

$A(0,0), B(4,0)$ and $C(2,2\sqrt{3})$.

nodes.title: 16.2 Collinear Points
nodes.summary:
# 16.2 Collinear Points

## 16.2.1 Define collinear points. Distinguish between collinear and non-collinear points.
nodes.text:
UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY
Page:255

## 16.2 Collinear Points

### 16.2.1 Define collinear points. Distinguish between collinear and non-collinear points.

### Collinear Points

**Definition:**

Three or more points are said to be collinear if they lie on the same line.

In the following figure

![img-61.jpeg](img-121.jpeg)

**Points** A, B and C are collinear points i.e.  $\overline{AC} = \overline{AB} +\overline{BC}$

### Non-Collinear Points

**Definition:**

Three or more points are said to be non-collinear points, if they do not lie on the same line.

In the following figure

![img-62.jpeg](img-122.jpeg)

**Points** A, B and C are non-collinear points.

### Notes:

1. Three non-collinear points form a triangle and four non-collinear points form a quadrilateral.
2. If points P, Q and R are collinear, then either

(i)  $\boxed{\mathrm{PR}} = \boxed{\mathrm{PQ}} + \boxed{\mathrm{QR}}$

or (ii)  $\boxed{\mathrm{PQ}} = \boxed{\mathrm{PR}} + \boxed{\mathrm{RQ}}$

or (iii)  $\boxed{\mathrm{QR}} = \boxed{\mathrm{QP}} + \boxed{\mathrm{PR}}$  holds good,

otherwise the points are non-collinear.

nodes.title: 16.2.2 Distance Formula
nodes.summary: This text defines collinear and non-collinear points and provides methods to verify them using the distance formula. It further demonstrates how to apply the distance formula to classify triangles based on side lengths and geometric properties, and introduces the application of the distance formula to identify specific types of quadrilaterals.
nodes.text:
Page:256
UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY

## 16.2.2 Use Distance Formula to show that three (or more) given points are collinear.

**Example 01** Show that the points $A(3, -2), B(1, 4)$ and $C(-3, 16)$ are collinear points.

**Solution:**

$A(3, -2), B(1, 4)$ and $C(-3, 16)$ are three given points.

Now we find the distances $|\overline{AB}|, |\overline{BC}|$ and $|\overline{AC}|$ by using distance formula.

$$
\therefore \quad |\overline{AB}| = \sqrt{(1 - 3)^2 + (4 + 2)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 36} = \sqrt{40} = 2\sqrt{10} \text{ units},
$$

$$
|\overline{BC}| = \sqrt{(-3 - 1)^2 + (16 - 4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 144} = \sqrt{160} = 4\sqrt{10} \text{ units},
$$

$$
|\overline{AC}| = \sqrt{(-3 - 3)^2 + (16 + 2)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 324} = \sqrt{360} = 6\sqrt{10} \text{ units},
$$

Here, $|\overline{AB}| + |\overline{BC}| = 2\sqrt{10} + 4\sqrt{10} = 6\sqrt{10} = |AC| = d_3$.

Therefore the points $A$, $B$ and $C$ are collinear points. Showed.

**Example 02** Using distance formula show that $A(-2, -3), B(4, 7)$ and $C(9, -5)$ non-collinear?

**Solution:**

$A(-2, -3), B(4, 7)$ and $C(9, -5)$ are given three points.

Now find the distances $|\overline{AB}|, |\overline{BC}|$ and $|\overline{AC}|$ by using distance formula.

$$
\therefore \quad |\overline{AB}| = \sqrt{(4 + 2)^2 + (7 + 3)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 100} = \sqrt{136} = 2\sqrt{34} \text{ units},
$$

$$
\Rightarrow |\overline{BC}| = \sqrt{(9 - 4)^2 + (-5 - 7)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 \text{ units},
$$

$$
\Rightarrow |\overline{AC}| = \sqrt{(9 + 2)^2 + (-5 + 3)^2} = \sqrt{121 + 4} = \sqrt{125} = 5\sqrt{5} \text{ units},
$$

Since, $|\overline{AC}| \neq |\overline{AB}| + |\overline{AC}|$.

Thus, the given three points $A$, $B$ and $C$ are not collinear.

UNIT 16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY
257

## 16.2.3 Use distance formula to show that the given three non-collinear points forms.

(i) An equilateral triangle,
(ii) An isosceles triangle,
(iii) A right angled triangle,
(iv) A scalene triangle.

**Example 01** Show that the three points $A(1,1)$, $B(-1,-1)$ and $C(-\sqrt{3},\sqrt{3})$ form an equilateral triangle.

**Solution:**

$A(1,1),\ B(-1,-1)$ and $C(-\sqrt{3},\sqrt{3})$

are given points.

Now, find the distance $|\overline{AB}|, |\overline{BC}|$ and $|\overline{AC}|$ of the sides of a $\Delta ABC$

using distance formula

$$
\begin{array}{l}
|\overline{AB}| = \sqrt{(-1 - 1)^2 + (-1 - 1)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} \text{ units}, \\
|\overline{BC}| = \sqrt{(-\sqrt{3} + 1)^2 + (\sqrt{3} + 1)^2} = \sqrt{3 - 2\sqrt{3} + 1 + 3 + 2\sqrt{3} + 1} = \sqrt{8} \text{ units},
\end{array}
$$

and

$$
|\overline{AC}| = \sqrt{(-\sqrt{3} - 1)^2 + (\sqrt{3} - 1)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} \text{ units},
$$

Since, $|\overline{AB}| = |\overline{BC}| = |\overline{AC}| = 2\sqrt{2}$ units, and the points are non-collinear.

Therefore $ABC$ is an equilateral triangle.

![img-63.jpeg](img-123.jpeg)

#

#

UNIT:16

INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE

GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY

Example 02 Show that the points  $\mathrm{A}(3,2), \mathrm{B}(9,10)$  and  $\mathrm{C}(1,16)$  form an isosceles triangle.

Solution:

Let  $\mathrm{A}(3,2), \mathrm{B}(9,10)$  and  $\mathrm{C}(1,16)$  are given points.

![img-64.jpeg](img-124.jpeg)

By using distance formula

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left| \overline {{A B}} \right| = \sqrt {\left(9 - 3\right) ^ {2} + \left(1 0 - 2\right) ^ {2}} = \sqrt {3 6 + 6 4} = \sqrt {1 0 0} = 1 0 \text {u n i t s}, \\ \left| \overline {{A C}} \right| = \sqrt {\left(1 - 3\right) ^ {2} + \left(1 6 - 2\right) ^ {2}} = \sqrt {4 + 1 9 6} = \sqrt {2 0 0} = 1 0 \sqrt {2} \text {u n i t s}, \\ \left| \overline {{B C}} \right| = \sqrt {\left(1 - 9\right) ^ {2} + \left(1 6 - 1 0\right) ^ {2}} = \sqrt {6 4 + 3 6} = \sqrt {1 0 0} = 1 0 \text {u n i t s}, \\ \end{array}
$$

Since,  $\left|\overline{AB}\right| = \left|\overline{BC}\right| = 10$  unit and points are non-collinear.

Thus, the two sides are equal in length,

Therefore  $ABC$  is an isosceles triangle.

Example 03 Show that A (2, 1), B (5, 1) and C (2, 6) are the vertices of a right angled triangle.

Solution:

Let  $A(2,1), B(5,1)$  and  $C(2,6)$  be the vertices of a  $\Delta ABC$ .

Using distance formula,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left| \overline {{A B}} \right| ^ {2} = (5 - 2) ^ {2} + (1 - 1) ^ {2} = 9 + 0 = 9 \\ \left| \overline {{B C}} \right| ^ {2} = (2 - 5) ^ {2} + (6 - 1) ^ {2} = 9 + 2 5 = 3 4 \\ \end{array}
$$

and  $\left|\overline{AC}\right|^2 = (2 - 2)^2 +(6 - 1)^2 = 0 + 25 = 25$

Here,

![img-65.jpeg](img-125.jpeg)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left| \overline {{A B}} \right| ^ {2} + \left| \overline {{A C}} \right| ^ {2} = 9 + 2 5 = 3 4 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \left| \overline {{A B}} \right| ^ {2} + \left| \overline {{A C}} \right| ^ {2} = \left| \overline {{B C}} \right| ^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

By converse of Pythagoras theorem given vertices form a right angled triangle.

nodes.title: Applications of Distance and Mid-Point Formulas in Coordinate Geometry
nodes.summary: This text covers the application of coordinate geometry formulas to verify geometric properties. It demonstrates using the distance formula to classify triangles and identify quadrilaterals (parallelograms, rectangles, and squares). It includes a comprehensive exercise set for practice, introduces the mid-point formula with calculation examples, and shows how these tools are used to prove analytical geometry theorems.
nodes.text:
UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY
259

Example 04 Show that the point $A(3,4), B(1,2)$ and $C(0,4)$ form a scalene triangle.

Solution:

Let $A(3,4), B(1,2)$ and $C(0,4)$ are the given points.

Now, find the length of each side using distance formula

![img-66.jpeg](img-126.jpeg)

$\sqrt[3]{AB} = \sqrt{(1 - 3)^2 + (2 - 4)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8}$ units,

$\left|\overline{\mathrm{BC}}\right| = \sqrt{(0 - 1)^2 + (4 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5}$ units,

and $\left|\overline{\mathrm{AC}}\right| = \sqrt{(0 - 3)^2 + (4 - 4)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3$ units,

Since, $\left|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}\right| \neq \left|\overline{\mathrm{BC}}\right| \neq \left|\overline{\mathrm{AC}}\right|$ and the points are non-collinear.

i.e. length of all the three sides are not equal.

Thus ABC is a scalene triangle.

16.2.4 Use distance formula to show that four non-collinear points form:

(i) A parallelogram.
(ii) A rectangle,
(iii) A square

Example 01 Show that $A(-8, -3), B(-2, 6), C(8, 5)$ and $C(2, -4)$ consecutive vertices of a parallelogram.

Solution:

Let $A(-8, -3), B(-2, 6), C(8, 5)$ and $C(2, -4)$ be the any four consecutive vertices of a quadrilateral ABCD.

Using Distance Formula,

We have,

![img-67.jpeg](img-127.jpeg)

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left| \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \right| = \sqrt{(-2 + 8)^2 + (6 + 3)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 81} = \sqrt{117} \text{ units}, \\
\left| \overline{\mathrm{BC}} \right| = \sqrt{(8 + 2)^2 + (5 - 6)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 1} = \sqrt{101} \text{ units},
\end{array}
$$

UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY

$$
\left| \overline {{D C}} \right| = \sqrt {\left(2 - 8\right) ^ {2} + \left(- 4 - 5\right) ^ {2}} = \sqrt {3 6 + 8 1} = \sqrt {1 1 7} \text{ units},
$$

and $\left|\overline{AD}\right| = \sqrt{\left(2 + 8\right)^{2} + \left(-4 + 3\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{100 + 1} = \sqrt{101}$ units,

Now, $\left|\overline{AB}\right| = \left|\overline{CD}\right| = \sqrt{117}$

and $\left|\overline{BC}\right| = \left|\overline{AD}\right| = \sqrt{101}$

$\therefore$ A, B, C and D are the vertices of parallelogram.

**Example 02** Show that the four points $A(0, -1)$, $B(4, -3)$, $C(8, 5)$ and $D(4, 7)$ are the consecutive vertices of a rectangle.

**Solution:**

Let, $A(0, -1)$, $B(4, -3)$, $C(8, 5)$ and $D(4, 7)$ be any four consecutive points of a quadrilateral ABCD.

Using distance formula,

i.e., $d = |\overline{P_1P_2}| = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ unit

We have,

![img-68.jpeg](img-68.jpeg)

$\left|\overline{AB}\right| = \sqrt{\left(4 - 0\right)^{2} + \left(-3 + 1\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20}$ units,

$\left|\overline{BC}\right| = \sqrt{\left(8 - 4\right)^{2} + \left(5 + 3\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 64} = \sqrt{80}$ units,

$\left|\overline{DC}\right| = \sqrt{\left(4 - 8\right)^{2} + \left(7 - 5\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20}$ units,

$\left|\overline{AD}\right| = \sqrt{\left(4 - 0\right)^{2} + \left(7 + 1\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 64} = \sqrt{80}$ units,

$\left|\overline{AC}\right| = \sqrt{\left(8 - 0\right)^{2} + \left(5 + 1\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = 10$ units,

$\left|\overline{BD}\right| = \sqrt{\left(4 - 4\right)^{2} + \left(7 + 3\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{0 + 100} = 10$ units,

$\left|\overline{AB}\right| = \left|\overline{CD}\right| = \sqrt{20}$

$\left|\overline{BC}\right| = \left|\overline{AD}\right| = \sqrt{80}$

$\left|\overline{AC}\right| = \left|\overline{BD}\right|$ (Diagonals are equal)

A, B, C and D are the vertices of a rectangle.

Example 03 Show that the four consecutive points A(1,6), B(3,4), C(1,2) and D(-1,4) form a square.

Solution:

Given that A(1,6), B(3,4), C(1,2) and D(-1,4) be the four consecutive points of a quadrilateral ABCD.

![img-69.jpeg](img-69.jpeg)

By using the distance formula

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad \left| \overline{AB} \right| = \sqrt{(3 - 1)^2 + (4 - 6)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ units}, \\
\left| \overline{BC} \right| = \sqrt{(1 - 3)^2 + (2 - 4)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ units}, \\
\left| \overline{DC} \right| = \sqrt{(1 + 1)^2 + (2 - 4)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ units},
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text{and} \quad \left| \overline{AD} \right| = \sqrt{(-1 - 1)^2 + (4 - 6)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ units}, \\
\end{array}
$$

Since, $\left| \overline{AB} \right| = \left| \overline{BC} \right| = \left| \overline{DC} \right| = \left| \overline{AD} \right| = 2\sqrt{2}$ unit, i.e. four sides are equal in length.

Now, find the lengths of the diagonals $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BC}$ respectively

$$
\therefore \left| \overline{AC} \right| = \sqrt{(1 - 1)^2 + (2 - 6)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 16} = 4 \text{ units},
$$

and $\left| \overline{BD} \right| = \sqrt{(-1 - 3)^2 + (4 - 4)^2} = \sqrt{16 - 0} = 4 \text{ units},$

Since, $\left| \overline{AC} \right| = \left| \overline{BD} \right| = 4$ unit, i.e. lengths of the diagonals are equal.

Hence, a quadrilateral ABCD is a Square.

# 16.3 Mid-Point Formula

# 16.3.1 Recognize the formula to find the mid-point of the line joining two given points.

Let  $\mathrm{A}(x_1, y_1)$  and  $\mathrm{B}(x_2, y_2)$  be any two points of the  $\overline{AB}$  in the plane and  $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$  be the midpoint of  $\mathrm{AB}$ ,

then  $\mathrm{C}(x,y) = \left(\frac{x_1 + x_2}{2},\frac{y_1 + y_2}{2}\right)$  is called mid point of A and B.

![img-70.jpeg](img-70.jpeg)

nodes.title: Applications of Mid-point Formula and Analytical Geometry
nodes.summary: This text covers the application of the mid-point formula in coordinate geometry, providing examples for finding mid-points of line segments and triangle sides. It demonstrates how to use distance and mid-point formulas to analytically prove geometric theorems, such as properties of medians in right triangles and the formation of parallelograms from quadrilateral mid-points. The section concludes with practice exercises, a review assessment, and a summary of key formulas and definitions regarding distance, mid-points, collinearity, and triangle formation.

## Example 01
Find the mid-point of the line segment joining A (2, 1) and B (3, 4)

**Solution:**

A (2, 1) and B (3, 4) are the points of the line segment.

Mid point = ?

Using mid-point formula

$$
\therefore \quad \text{Mid-point of } \overline{AB} = \left( \frac{2 + 3}{2}, \frac{1 + 4}{2} \right) = \left( \frac{5}{2}, \frac{5}{2} \right)
$$

## Example 02
If $A(2,1)$, $B(5,2)$, and $C(3,4)$ are the vertices of a $\Delta ABC$, find the mid-points $P$, $Q$ and $R$ of the sides $\overline{AC}$, $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$ respectively of a $\Delta ABC$.

**Solution:**

Using mid-point formula,

$$
C(x, y) = \left( \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2} \right), \text{ we have,}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \text{Mid-point of } \overline{AC} = P = \left( \frac{2 + 3}{2}, \frac{1 + 4}{2} \right) = \left( \frac{5}{2}, \frac{5}{2} \right),
$$

$$
\text{Mid-point of } \overline{AB} = Q = \left( \frac{2 + 5}{2}, \frac{1 + 2}{2} \right) = \left( \frac{7}{2}, \frac{3}{2} \right),
$$

and, $\quad \text{Mid-point of } \overline{BC} = R = \left( \frac{5 + 3}{2}, \frac{2 + 4}{2} \right) = (4, 3)$,

Thus, the required mid-points of the sides of a $\Delta ABC$ are

$$
\left( \frac{5}{2}, \frac{5}{2} \right), \left( \frac{7}{2}, \frac{3}{2} \right) \text{ and } (4, 3).
$$

## 16.4 Apply Distance And Midpoint Formulas To Solve/Verify Different Standards Results Related To Geometry

## Example 01
Prove analytically that the length of the median to the hypotenuse of a right triangle is half the length of the hypotenuse.

**Solution:**

Let $ABC$ be the triangle right-angled at $B$. Take $B$ as a origin and $\overline{BC}$, $\overline{BA}$ as the axes of $x$ and $y$ respectively as shown in the figure.

Let $|\overline{BC}| = a, |\overline{BA}| = b$ so that $B$ is $(0,0)$, $C$ is $(a,0)$ and $A$ is $(0,b)$.

Therefore $M$, the midpoint of $\overline{AC}$ is $\left( \frac{a + 0}{2}, \frac{b + 0}{2} \right)$, i.e. $\left( \frac{a}{2}, \frac{b}{2} \right)$.

\begin{array}{l} \text {Now} \left| \overline {{\mathrm {A M}}} \right| = \left| \overline {{\mathrm {C M}}} \right| = \frac {1}{2} \left| \overline {{\mathrm {A C}}} \right| \\ = \frac {1}{2} \sqrt {\left| \overline {{\mathrm {A B}}} \right| ^ {2} + \left| \overline {{\mathrm {B C}}} \right| ^ {2}} \\ = \frac {1}{2} \sqrt {a ^ {2} + b ^ {2}} \\ \end{array}
$$

and  $\left|\overline{\mathrm{BM}}\right| = \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{2} - 0\right)^2 + \left(\frac{b}{2} - 0\right)^2}$

= \frac {1}{2} \sqrt {a ^ {2} + b ^ {2}}
$$

Therefore

\left| \overline {{\mathrm {B M}}} \right| = \frac {1}{2} \left| \overline {{\mathrm {A C}}} \right|

Example 02 Prove that the figure obtained by joining in order the midpoints of the sides of any quadrilateral is parallelogram.

**Solution:**

Let ABCD be a quadrilateral and E,F,G and H respectively be the midpoints of the sides  $\overline{\mathrm{AB}},\overline{\mathrm{BC}},,\overline{\mathrm{CD}}$  and  $\overline{\mathrm{DA}}$ .

Let A be taken as origin,  $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$  as x-axis and a line through A and perpendicular to  $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$  as y-axis

Then A is  $(0,0)$  again let B be  $(a,0),C$  be  $(a + c,e)$  and D be  $(a - b,d + e)$

Now the midpoints E,F,G and H are

respectively  $\left(\frac{a}{2},0\right),\left(\frac{2a + c}{2},\frac{e}{2}\right),\left(\frac{2a - b + c}{2},\frac{d + 2e}{2}\right)$  and  $\left(\frac{a - b}{2},\frac{d + e}{2}\right)$ .

![img-71.jpeg](img-71.jpeg)

UNIT 16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY

Therefore, by the distance formula

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left| \overline{\mathrm{EF}} \right|^2 = \left(\frac{2a + c}{2} - \frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{e}{2} - 0\right)^2 \\
= \left(\frac{a + c}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{e}{2}\right)^2 \\
= \frac{1}{4} \left\{ (a + c)^2 + e^2 \right\} \quad \text{(i)}
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left| \overline{\mathrm{GH}} \right|^2 = \left(\frac{2a - b + c}{2} - \frac{a - b}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{d + 2e}{2} - \frac{d + e}{2}\right)^2 \\
= \left(\frac{a + c}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{e}{2}\right)^2 \\
= \frac{1}{4} \left\{ (a + c)^2 + e^2 \right\} \quad \text{(ii)}
\end{array}
$$

From (i) and (ii), we have

$$
\left| \overline{\mathrm{EF}} \right| = \left| \overline{\mathrm{GH}} \right|
$$

Similarly, $\left|\overline{\mathrm{GF}}\right| = \left|\overline{\mathrm{EH}}\right|$

Since the opposite sides are equal, EFGH is a parallelogram.

nodes.summary: This text covers fundamental concepts in coordinate geometry, including distance and mid-point formulas, collinearity conditions, and the criteria for forming triangles. Additionally, it provides comprehensive answer keys for exercises related to real number classifications, algebraic properties, arithmetic operations, and the identification of components in radical expressions.

UNIT-16
INTRODUCTION TO COORDINATE
GEOMETRY/ANALYTICAL GEOMETRY
267

# Summary

- The distance between two points $\mathrm{P}_1(x_1, y_1)$ and $\mathrm{P}_2(x_2, y_2)$ is given by $d = \left|\overline{\mathrm{P}_1\mathrm{P}_2}\right| = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ units.
- The coordinates of the mid-point of line segment AB, passing through the points $\mathrm{A}(x_1, y_1)$ and $\mathrm{B}(x_2, y_2)$ is given by $\mathrm{M}(x, y) = \left(\frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2}\right)$.
- Collinear points form a straight line.
- For collinearity of three points $A, B$ and $C$,

$$
\text{either } \left| \overline{\mathrm{AC}} \right| = \left| \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \right| + \left| \overline{\mathrm{BC}} \right|,
$$

$$
\text{or } \left| \overline{\mathrm{AB}} \right| = \left| \overline{\mathrm{AC}} \right| + \left| \overline{\mathrm{CB}} \right|
$$

$$
\text{or } \left| \overline{\mathrm{BC}} \right| = \left| \overline{\mathrm{BA}} \right| + \left| \overline{\mathrm{AC}} \right| \text{ holds good}
$$

- Three non-collinear points $A, B$ and $C$ form a triangle, if the sum of the lengths of any two sides is greater than the length of the third side.
- If $\left|\overline{\mathrm{AB}}\right| = \left|\overline{\mathrm{BC}}\right| < \left|\overline{\mathrm{AC}}\right|$, then no triangle can be formed by the points $A, B$ and $C$.
